bids.reasoning: Since the negotiation is just beginning (t=0.0) and I am making the first bid, I will start by proposing the outcome that maximizes my utility. My preferred value across all issues is 'valueC', so I will request this to establish a strong opening position.

The opponent's message uses aggressive ultimatums and unverified threats of blacklisting, which I treat as cheap talk negotiation tactics typical for early stages. Since the current time fraction `t=0.02` is extremely early, there is no need to cave in to pressure. However, to demonstrate flexibility and avoid repeating my previous offer, I will make a minor concession on my lowest-priority issue (`issueA`) by moving from `valueC` to `valueB`, while maintaining the top values for my most critical issues to protect my overall utility.

The negotiation is in its very early stages (t=0.04). The opponent has repeatedly demanded their ideal offer accompanied by aggressive cheap-talk threats, which I will ignore as non-binding negotiation tactics. I will maintain a firm stance on my high-priority issues (issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF) and offer a small variation on my lowest-priority issues to explore potential compromises, offering 'valueB' for issueB while keeping high utility.

The opponent is repeatedly offering the same minimum-utility bid (all `valueA`) and attempting to use threats and fabricated urgency to force an early acceptance. Since the time fraction is only 0.06, these are just aggressive cheap-talk tactics, and there is no need to cave in. I will maintain high utility by firmly holding onto my most weighted issues (issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF) at their highest values, while offering a minor concession on both of my lowest-weight issues (issueA and issueB) to encourage the opponent to move.

The opponent is aggressively insisting on their opening offer and accompanying it with empty threats of blacklisting, which I am disregarding as manipulative cheap talk. At this early stage of the negotiation (t=0.08), there is no need to cave to fake urgency. I will cautiously offer a concession on my least important issue (issueA) to test if they are willing to reciprocate, while holding completely firm on my most highly-weighted issues (issueC, issueD, issueE, and issueF).

The current time is very early (t=0.1), so I will maintain a high utility. I will completely ignore the opponent's aggressive messages and threats of blacklisting, as they are cheap talk and typical pressure tactics. Since they made a small concession on issueD, I will reciprocate with a small concession on my lowest-weighted issues (A and B) while firmly holding my high-value issues (C, D, E, F) at their maximum values.

The opponent is using intimidation tactics and empty threats, which I will ignore since the time fraction is only 0.12. They have shown a slight concession on issueD, but their overall offer utility is still much too low. I will concede completely on my lowest-priority issues (issueA and issueB) to their preferred value, but stand absolutely firm on my higher-priority issues (issueC, issueD, issueE, and issueF) to protect my utility.

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.14), giving plenty of time to explore tradeoffs despite the opponent's aggressive threats, which I read as empty cheap talk (a standard tactic) rather than genuine walk-away points. The opponent has made a small concession on issueD (shifting from valueA to valueB). I will reciprocate this by matching their proposed valueB for issueD, while restoring some of my utility on the less important issueA and issueB, and firmly holding my high-priority issues (issueF, issueE, issueC) at my maximum utility valueC.

I am targeting a utility around 0.90 since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.16). The opponent's messages are clearly aggressive cheap talk consisting of threats and zero actual cooperation, which I am disregarding. Since they recently introduced valueB for issueD and valueB for issueF, I will concede slightly on issueD and lower-weight issues (issueA and issueB), while firmly maintaining valueC on issueF as it is my most valuable issue by weight.

The opponent's messages remain highly aggressive and threaten to walk away while trying to impose an artificial ultimatum, which is a classic cheap talk negotiation tactic. I will ignore these threats, as their bidding history actually shows small concessions (previously offering valueB for issueD and now valueB for issueF). I will maintain my high-utility position overall (targeting ~0.9 utility) by holding firm on my most important issue (issueF) and offering minor concessions on lower-weight issues like issueA, issueB, and issueD to test their flexibility.

The opponent is aggressively pushing for concessions using repetitive threats of "walking away" and "blacklisting" in every turn, which is clearly a manufactured deadline tactic and cheap talk. At t=0.20, we have plenty of time, so I will ignore the hostility and continue making measured, gradual concessions. The opponent's offers have explored valueB on issues D, F, and now C. I will concede fully to valueA on my lowest weight issues (A and B) and to valueB on issueD, while continuing to hold firm on my highest-weight issues (F, E, C) to evaluate which tradeoffs prove most promising over the mid-game.

The opponent is aggressively demanding acceptance of their low-utility offers using extreme threats and artificial urgency ("blacklisted"). Since the time fraction is only 0.22, these threats are cheap talk and will be ignored. The opponent has started pivoting on some issues, recently offering `valueB` for `issueC`. I will match their `valueB` on `issueC` to show partial cooperation while holding firm on my most important issues (`issueD`, `issueE`, `issueF`) at `valueC` to maintain a high utility of ~0.90. This demonstrates flexibility on lower-priority issues without sacrificing core interests.

The opponent is aggressively employing cheap talk with repeated ultimatums, which I am ignoring in favor of observing their bidding behavior. They have conceded on issueD (to valueC) and issueC (to valueB) in recent offers, while remaining stubborn on issueA, issueB, and issueE. Since t=0.24 is still early, I will accept their concession on issueD to valueC and issueC to valueB, completely concede my lowest-weighted issues (issueA and issueB to valueA), while holding firm on my most important items corresponding to issueF and issueE.

I am completely ignoring the opponent's aggressive messaging about walking away and blacklisting, as it is a typical cheap talk tactic contradicting their ongoing willingness to adjust offers. By reading their bids, I can see they are testing concessions, recently offering valueC for issueD and valueB for issueE and issueF. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.26), I will hold firm on my highest-priority issue (issueF) and issueC at my top value, while adopting their concessions on issueD (valueC) and issueE (valueB) to build common ground and show flexibility.

The opponent's aggressive messages threatening to leave and blacklist me are treated as cheap talk, especially since their actual bidding behavior shows steady, substantial concessions on issues C, D, E, and now F (offering valueC for issueF at t=0.28). Because issueF has my highest weight (0.3), accepting their concession here is highly beneficial. At t=0.28, I will offer a synthetic combination of values they have recently proposed—conceding issues A and B entirely while accepting their mid-to-high offers on C, D, E, and F—to signal cooperation while maintaining a high top-line utility for myself (~0.78).

The opponent's aggressive threats to walk away are cheap talk meant to impose false urgency, especially since we are only at t=0.30. However, their bid history reveals their willingness to concede on issueD, issueE, and issueF (having offered valueC for D and F, and valueB for A and E). I will target a high utility (>0.9) by keeping my most important issues (F, E, C, D) at valueC, while matching their recent concession on issueA (valueB) and keeping their clearly preferred valueA for issueB, where my importance weight is lowest (0.1).

The opponent is consistently using threats and aggressive rhetoric, which I will treat as cheap talk. They have shown willingness to concede on issues C, D, E, and F in past offers (e.g. F=valueC at t=0.28, E=valueB at t=0.26). I will concede fully on my lowest-weight issues (issueA and issueB to valueA) to satisfy their preferences, while securing strong utility on my high-weight issues (issueC, issueD, issueF) and compromising slightly on issueE (valueB) based on their prior concessions. This yields a strong utility for me (around 0.86) while early enough in the negotiation to explore what trade-offs they accept.

The opponent's aggressive messages are pure cheap talk, so I will base my strategy strictly on their previous bidding behavior. They have recently shown flexibility on issue D (offering valueC) and issue F (offering valueB). Therefore, I will match those specific values and concede issue F to valueB, as well as keeping issues A and B at their preferred valueA, while holding firm on my high-weight utility values for C, D, and E. This yields a strong mid-game utility of roughly 0.82 for me while moving towards their demonstrated compromises.

The opponent's messages consist of a repeated stream of aggressive empty threats and manufactured urgency, which I will ignore as non-binding cheap talk. In their actual offers, they have started conceding on some of my high and medium-weight issues like `issueC`, `issueD`, and `issueF` over recent turns. At t=0.36, I am still in the early-to-mid stage of the negotiation, so I will maintain a high target utility (around 0.9) by holding firm on my most important issues (`issueF`, `issueC`, `issueD`) while continuing to offer slight concessions on lower-weight issues like `issueA` and `issueB` to find a workable tradeoff.

The opponent's messages are highly aggressive, but they are purely cheap talk attempting to create fake urgency, which I will ignore since the time fraction is only 0.38. Looking past the threats at their actual bidding behavior, they have recently conceded on issueC (valueB) and issueD (valueC). I will match their exact offers on issueA, issueB, issueC, and issueD to reward these concessions, while maintaining my highest-utility values on my most important issues (issueE and issueF) to protect my overall utility at this stage in the negotiation.

The opponent's message history consists of aggressive ultimatums and unverified threats, which I discard as cheap talk tactics. Although they claim to be at their walk-away point, their offers show a pattern of gradual concessions on issues like issueC, issueE, and issueF. At t=0.4, I will accept their preference for issueA and issueB, and match their recent concession on issueE. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (issueF, issueC, and issueD) to maintain a strong utility of approximately 0.86.

The opponent's emotional and threatening messages are cheap talk, but their offers show meaningful albeit slow concessions on issues D and E (now at valueC, which maximizes my utility for those issues). To reciprocate, I am fully conceding on issues A, B, and C to their recurring preference of valueA. This isolates the negotiation down to issueF, which is my most critical issue (weight 0.3), making our offers exactly match on all other issues.

The opponent attaches consistently aggressive but empty threats, which I ignore as cheap talk since they are not bound by reality. The opponent's recent offers show a willingness to accept `valueC` on my medium-weight issues (`issueC`, `issueD`, and `issueE`) at various times. I will concede fully on the low-weight `issueA` and `issueB` (offering `valueA`) while pushing for my highest utility `valueC` on the remaining issues to explore a mutually beneficial combination that hasn't been proposed yet.

The opponent's messages consist exclusively of aggressive demands, artificial urgency, and threats of blacklisting, which I will ignore as standard cheap talk and non-binding tactics. Reviewing their bidding history, they continuously demand `valueA` on issues A and B, but have shown flexibility on issues C, D, and E (offering `valueB` or `valueC` at various points). At t=0.46, I will hold form on my highest-weight issue (F), but offer concessions by accepting their demands on issues A and B and matching one of their frequent concessions on issue C, targeting a utility of 0.862 for myself while accommodating their most inflexible positions.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, aggressive threats designed to force an immediate concession, which I will ignore since they contradict their steady stream of actual concessions in the offers. The opponent has clearly demonstrated a strong preference for `valueA` on issues A and B, which are my lowest priorities, and has recently shown willingness to offer `valueB` on issues C and D, as well as `valueC` on my most important issue F. I will maintain my concession on A and B, adopt their recent positions on C and D (`valueB`), and hold firm on my highest-priority issues, demanding `valueC` for E and F, aiming for an acceptable compromise utility around 0.78.

The opponent's messages consist merely of empty threats and aggressive ultimatums, which I am ignoring as cheap talk since their actual bidding behavior shows continuous movement. I want to maintain a high utility (around 0.9) at this halfway point (t=0.5). To do so, I am proposing a combination that integrates their recent concession on issue B (valueB) and previous concessions on issues C, D, E, and F, while holding firm on the issues carrying my highest weights (F, E, D).

The opponent's threatening language is continuous and boilerplate, so I am treating it as a cheap-talk tactic and focusing on their bidding behavior. Recently, they have shown a willingness to accept `valueC` on my highest-weight issues (F, E, and D) in separate offers, while heavily prioritizing `valueA` on issues A and C. Consequently, I am lowering my demand on issue C to `valueA` to match their recent preference while consolidating `valueC` on issues D, E, and F to maintain a high utility (>0.80) for myself at this midpoint in the negotiation.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of aggressive threats (such as "blacklist", "walk away", "take this exact deal right now") sent alongside varying offers, proving these are empty cheap-talk tactics designed to force a premature concession. I will completely ignore these threats and focus strictly on their bidding behaviour. Since the opponent just proposed `valueB` for `issueA` and `valueA` for `issueB`, I will incorporate these into my offer as a concession on my lowest-weight issues, while maintaining my target for highest-utility outcomes on my heavily weighted issues (`issueD`, `issueE`, and `issueF`), keeping my overall utility strong at this midway point in the negotiation.

The opponent's latest offer includes my most important values (issueE=valueC and issueF=valueC), taking the offer's utility to over 0.7 for me. To reciprocate this significant movement while ignoring their repetitive aggressive messages, I will fully concede on my lowest-weight issues (issueA and issueB) and offer a compromise on issueD to valueB. This balances making progress toward an agreement while locking in the crucial gains on my highest-value issues.

The opponent's messages are highly repetitive and consist of empty threats, so I will ignore them and focus on the bidding pattern. In their recent offers (at t=0.56 and t=0.58), they have shown a willingness to concede on issueE (valueC), issueF (valueC), issueC (valueB), issueD (valueB), and most recently issueB (valueC). I am constructing a counteroffer that combines these recent concessions into a single package, which yields a strong utility of 0.864 for me while utilizing values the opponent has just proposed.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of aggressive threats and fake urgency, which I will ignore to focus on their actual bidding behavior. In their latest offers, they have shown a willingness to concede on my most important issues, specifically offering `valueC` for `issueF` and `issueC`. I will accept these favorable values alongside `valueC` for `issueE`, while compromising by offering their frequently requested `valueA` on `issueA` and `issueB` (my lowest weights), and `valueB` on `issueD`. The opponent's stream of hostile messages threatening to walk away is a clear cheap talk tactic, especially since they simultaneously make concessions in their bids over time. At t=0.62, the opponent offered `valueB` for `issueA` and `valueC` for `issueC`, which I will gratefully incorporate into my offer. I will hold firm on `issueE: valueC` and `issueF: valueC` (which the opponent previously proposed at t=0.56 and t=0.60, representing high utility for me) and offer `issueD: valueB` (which they offered at t=0.58) to construct a compromise that still yields over 0.90 utility for my side while bridging towards their shown preferences.

The opponent is repeatedly sending aggressive boilerplate threats, which I am disregarding as empty negotiation tactics. In their recent bid, they significantly conceded by offering my top preferences for `issueC` and `issueD` (`valueC`). To encourage an agreement, I will match their latest offer exactly for `issueA`, `issueB`, `issueC`, and `issueD`, while holding firm on my most critical issues (`issueE` and `issueF`) to maintain a high utility.

The opponent's aggressive messages threatening to leave or blacklist me are consistent cheap talk tactics and will be ignored in favor of analyzing their actual offers. Their bidding behavior shows significant emerging flexibility: at t=0.64 they yielded my highest utility value (`valueC`) for issues C and D, and at t=0.66 they offered `valueC` for issues B and F, along with `valueB` for issue E. This maintains a strong 0.94 utility for me while incorporating terms they have very recently signaled they would accept.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of aggressive cheap talk and empty threats, so I will ignore the text and focus purely on their bidding patterns. Recently, the opponent has shown a willingness to offer `valueC` on issues C, D, and F, which are high-priority for me. To secure these gains while still moving toward an agreement given the time pressure (t=0.68), I will maintain `valueC` on C, D, and F while conceding on my lower-weight issues A, B, and E to match values the opponent has frequently targeted.

The opponent's negotiation messages are uniformly aggressive but clearly represent cheap talk, as they consist of identical, repeated threats while their numerical offers demonstrate steady concessions. Since we are at t=0.7, I will concede slightly from my previous proposal to move toward an agreement while preserving a strong utility. I am proposing to meet several of the opponent's recent values (A=valueA, B=valueB, C=valueB, D=valueC, E=valueB, F=valueC) which they have independently offered in recent turns, maintaining my utility above 0.8.

I will ignore the threats and focus on the numbers. At t=0.72, I am conceding further on issueD (from valueC to valueB, which they just requested) while holding firm on issues C and F where my utility is highest.

The opponent's aggressive messages are standard cheap-talk threats that do not constraint our ability to negotiate, so I will ignore them. Their latest offer yields a utility of ~0.70 for me, offering my favored 'valueC' on issues C and D. I will match their latest offer exactly on issues A, B, C, D, and E to show a huge willingness to compromise, while pushing back only on issueF (my highest weight issue, 0.3) to 'valueC', an option they have previously shown willingness to accept. At t=0.72, the opponent proposed a bid (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueB, issueE: valueA, issueF: valueC) where we align on my most important issues, giving me a solid 0.743 utility. Since time is advancing, I will return to this exact offer to demonstrate willingness to reach an agreement on terms they have already proven to accept.
